merged_findings: There is a strong and consistent distributional shift in the Destination Port feature between BENIGN and DDoS classes, characterized by a dominant single value and zero entropy in the DDoS class, contrasting with higher cardinality and entropy in the BENIGN class.
High Jensen-Shannon divergence (0.7315) quantitatively confirms this significant distributional shift.

The near-deterministic mapping of Destination Port values to class labels in the DDoS class suggests strong class separation and predictive leverage of this feature alone within the local evidence scope (e33).
Local consistency checks reveal high label neighborhood conflicts and duplicate label conflicts, reinforcing the abruptness and strength of the distributional shift.
No contradictions were observed across the worker results within the local evidence scope.
open_gaps: Analyses are limited to the single feature Destination Port within localized evidence e33; broader context, multi-feature interactions, and global aggregation remain unassessed.
update_focus: Maintain focus on the strong local distributional shift in Destination Port between BENIGN and DDoS classes, emphasizing its near-deterministic predictive role and the need to explore broader feature interactions and global context in subsequent rounds.
